steps.title: What removing a value does to mean and median
steps.content:
The original data set has 11 values. The new data set has 10 values after removing $78.2^{\circ}\text{F}$. Track the two measures separately:
  • Mean: removing a value above the old mean lowers the mean.
  • Median: the old median is the 6th sorted value; after removing the largest value, the new median is the average of the old 5th and 6th sorted values.

steps.title: Apply to this histogram
steps.content:
The histogram gives two quick checks:
  • The removed value, $78.2^{\circ}\text{F}$, is the high outlier, so it is above the original mean. Removing it must lower the mean. Statement I is true.
  • The histogram does not force the old 5th and 6th values to be different. If those two values are equal, then averaging them gives the same median as before. Statement II is not guaranteed.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• A: I only — matches: removing the maximum 78.2 always lowers the mean, but the median need not strictly decrease.
  • B: II only — wrong because the mean must decrease (78.2 exceeds the original mean).
  • C: I and II — wrong because II is not forced: the 5th and 6th sorted values can be equal, leaving the median unchanged.
  • D: neither — wrong because I is always true.
A is correct.
